title: Artificial Neural Network
definition: A computational model inspired by biological neural networks. Consists of interconnected layers of neurons (nodes): input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Data flows forward through the network with weighted connections and non-linear activation functions.
why_used: Foundation of all deep learning. Can approximate any continuous function (Universal Approximation Theorem) given sufficient depth and width. Learns complex hierarchical representations from raw data.
interview_point: Universal Approximation Theorem: A feedforward network with at least one hidden layer and a non-linear activation can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary accuracy. The weights are learned via backpropagation + gradient descent.
limitations: Requires large amounts of data
Black-box nature
Computationally expensive
Many hyperparameters to tune
Prone to vanishing/exploding gradients
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class ANN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dims, output_dim):
        super().__init__()
        layers = []
        in_dim = input_dim
        for h in hidden_dims:
            layers.extend([nn.Linear(in_dim, h), nn.ReLU(), nn.BatchNorm1d(h), nn.Dropout(0.3)])
            in_dim = h
        layers.append(nn.Linear(in_dim, output_dim))
        self.net = nn.Sequential(*layers)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

model = ANN(input_dim=128, hidden_dims=[256, 128, 64], output_dim=10)
```

title: Multilayer Perceptron
definition: A fully connected feedforward neural network with multiple hidden layers. Every neuron in one layer connects to every neuron in the next layer. Uses backpropagation and gradient descent for training.
why_used: Standard architecture for tabular data tasks in deep learning. Learns complex feature interactions through deep layers of transformations.
interview_point: MLP suffers from the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks — gradients become extremely small as they backpropagate through many layers. Solutions: ReLU activations, Batch Normalization, residual connections, and careful weight initialization (Xavier/He init).
limitations: Slow on tabular data vs gradient boosting
Requires careful hyperparameter tuning
Vanishing gradient problem in deep networks
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier

# sklearn's MLP (good for quick experiments)
model = MLPClassifier(
    hidden_layer_sizes=(256, 128, 64),
    activation='relu',
    solver='adam',
    alpha=0.001,        # L2 regularization
    batch_size=64,
    learning_rate_init=0.001,
    max_iter=300,
    early_stopping=True,
    random_state=42
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

title: ResNet
definition: A deep CNN architecture introduced in 2015 by Microsoft Research that uses residual connections (skip connections) to add the input of a block directly to its output. This solves the vanishing gradient problem, enabling training of very deep networks (50-152+ layers).
why_used: ResNets enabled dramatically deeper networks without degradation in training accuracy. ResNet50 and ResNet152 are foundational backbones for countless computer vision tasks.
interview_point: The core innovation: the residual block computes H(x) = F(x) + x instead of H(x) = F(x). If the optimal function is close to the identity, the network only needs to learn F(x) ≈ 0, which is much easier. Skip connections also provide gradient highways during backpropagation.
advantages: Won ILSVRC 2015 with top-5 error of 3.57%
limitations: High parameter count (ResNet50: 25M params)
Computationally expensive
Still relatively large for mobile deployment
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torchvision.models as models
from torch import nn

# Load pretrained ResNet50
model = models.resnet50(weights='IMAGENET1K_V2')

# Freeze backbone, fine-tune classifier head
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace final classification layer
num_features = model.fc.in_features
model.fc = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Dropout(0.5),
    nn.Linear(num_features, num_classes)
)

# Only train the classifier
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.fc.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
```

title: EfficientNet
definition: A family of CNN architectures (B0-B7) by Google Brain that scales width, depth, and resolution using a compound coefficient. Instead of scaling one dimension, compound scaling balances all three for maximum efficiency.
why_used: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly fewer parameters than ResNet. EfficientNet-B4 outperforms ResNet-50 with 5x fewer parameters.
interview_point: Compound Scaling: EfficientNet scales depth (d), width (w), and resolution (r) simultaneously using a compound coefficient φ: d = α^φ, w = β^φ, r = γ^φ, where α·β²·γ² ≈ 2. This was found via a grid search on the scaling coefficients.
advantages: Multiple scales (B0-B7) for different compute budgets
limitations: Complex architecture
Slower training than simpler models
EfficientNetV2 recommended over original for most tasks
real_world_example:
```python
import torchvision.models as models
import torch.nn as nn

# Use EfficientNet-B4 for good accuracy/efficiency balance
model = models.efficientnet_b4(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')

# Fine-tune: freeze all but classifier
for param in model.features.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

num_features = model.classifier[1].in_features
model.classifier = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Dropout(0.4),
    nn.Linear(num_features, num_classes)
)
```

title: YOLOv8
definition: The 8th generation of the YOLO (You Only Look Once) family by Ultralytics. A single-stage object detector that processes the entire image in a single forward pass, producing bounding boxes and class predictions simultaneously.
why_used: State-of-the-art real-time object detection. Supports detection, segmentation, classification, pose estimation, and tracking in a unified framework with an easy-to-use API.
interview_point: YOLO divides the image into a grid and predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities for each grid cell simultaneously. YOLOv8 uses an anchor-free approach (unlike v5), a CSP backbone, and decoupled classification/regression heads.
advantages: Single model supports multiple vision tasks
limitations: Struggles with very small objects
Accuracy can fall below two-stage detectors on dense scenes
Requires GPU for training
real_world_example:
```python
from ultralytics import YOLO

# Load pretrained model
model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')  # n=nano, s=small, m=medium, l=large, x=xlarge

# Inference on image
results = model('image.jpg', conf=0.25, iou=0.45)

# Fine-tune on custom data
model.train(
    data='custom_data.yaml',  # Dataset config
    epochs=100,
    imgsz=640,
    batch=16,
    lr0=0.01,
    project='runs/detect',
    name='custom_model'
)

# Export to ONNX for deployment
model.export(format='onnx')
```

title: Faster R-CNN
definition: A two-stage object detection architecture. Stage 1: A Region Proposal Network (RPN) generates candidate bounding box proposals. Stage 2: A classification network (Fast R-CNN head) refines the boxes and classifies each proposal.
why_used: Higher accuracy than one-stage detectors on complex scenes with occluded or small objects. The gold standard for accuracy-focused object detection tasks.
interview_point: The key innovation of Faster R-CNN over Fast R-CNN: replacing the selective search proposal method with a learned Region Proposal Network (RPN), making the entire pipeline end-to-end trainable. The RPN shares convolutional features with the detection head via a shared backbone.
advantages: Works well on small and occluded objects
limitations: Slow inference (~5 FPS) — not suitable for real-time
Complex training pipeline
High computational cost
real_world_example:
```python
import torchvision
from torchvision.models.detection import fasterrcnn_resnet50_fpn
from torchvision.models.detection.faster_rcnn import FastRCNNPredictor

# Load pretrained
model = fasterrcnn_resnet50_fpn(weights='DEFAULT')

# Replace box predictor for custom classes
in_features = model.roi_heads.box_predictor.cls_score.in_features
model.roi_heads.box_predictor = FastRCNNPredictor(in_features, num_classes + 1)

# Inference (model in eval mode)
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    predictions = model([image_tensor])
    boxes = predictions[0]['boxes']
    scores = predictions[0]['scores']
    labels = predictions[0]['labels']
```

title: U-Net
definition: An encoder-decoder CNN architecture with skip connections between corresponding encoder and decoder layers, forming a 'U' shape. Designed for biomedical image segmentation where precise pixel-level predictions are required.
why_used: Works extremely well with small datasets (biomedical setting), produces pixel-level masks, and skip connections preserve fine-grained spatial details lost during downsampling.
interview_point: Skip connections in U-Net concatenate (not add) encoder feature maps to decoder feature maps. This allows the decoder to use both high-level semantic information (from bottleneck) and precise spatial details (from encoder) simultaneously.
advantages: Excellent with small datasets due to data augmentation
limitations: Originally designed for 2D images (3D U-Net for volumes)
Can be slow without GPU
Requires annotated pixel-level masks
real_world_example:
```python
import segmentation_models_pytorch as smp

# Simple U-Net with pretrained encoder
model = smp.Unet(
    encoder_name='resnet34',      # Encoder backbone
    encoder_weights='imagenet',   # Pretrained on ImageNet
    in_channels=3,                # RGB input
    classes=1,                    # Binary segmentation
    activation='sigmoid'
)

# Loss function for segmentation
loss_fn = smp.losses.DiceLoss(mode='binary')

# Training step
output = model(images)     # (B, 1, H, W)
loss = loss_fn(output, masks)
```

title: Long Short-Term Memory
definition: A specialized RNN architecture with gated memory cells that can selectively remember and forget information over long sequences. Three gates: Forget Gate (what to discard), Input Gate (what to store), Output Gate (what to output).
why_used: Solves the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs, enabling learning of long-range temporal dependencies in sequences. Foundation for many NLP and time series tasks.
interview_point: The three gates: Forget Gate uses sigmoid to decide what to delete from cell state. Input Gate uses sigmoid (what to update) + tanh (candidate values) to add new information. Output Gate uses sigmoid to control what part of cell state becomes hidden state. Cell state is the 'conveyor belt' that preserves long-term memory.
limitations: Computationally expensive (sequential processing)
Largely superseded by Transformers for NLP
Difficult to parallelize
Still struggles with very long sequences
formula: fₜ = σ(Wf·[hₜ₋₁, xₜ] + bf)  |  iₜ = σ(Wi·[hₜ₋₁, xₜ] + bi)  |  cₜ = fₜ⊙cₜ₋₁ + iₜ⊙tanh(Wc·[hₜ₋₁, xₜ])
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LSTMModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, output_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(
            input_size=input_size,
            hidden_size=hidden_size,
            num_layers=num_layers,
            dropout=0.2,
            batch_first=True,    # Input: (batch, seq, features)
            bidirectional=False  # Set True for BiLSTM
        )
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, input_size)
        out, (hn, cn) = self.lstm(x)
        return self.fc(out[:, -1, :])  # Last timestep output
```

title: Gated Recurrent Unit
definition: A simplified variant of LSTM with two gates (Reset Gate and Update Gate) instead of three. Merges the cell state and hidden state. Generally comparable to LSTM but computationally more efficient.
why_used: Fewer parameters than LSTM while achieving similar performance. Trains faster and is less prone to overfitting on smaller datasets.
interview_point: GRU vs LSTM: GRU has 2 gates (update, reset) vs LSTM's 3 (forget, input, output). GRU combines cell state and hidden state. GRU trains faster with fewer parameters. LSTM may outperform on very long sequences. In practice, performance is often similar — try both.
limitations: Slightly less expressive than LSTM
Still sequential (can't parallelize)
Superseded by Transformers for most NLP
real_world_example:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

class GRUModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, output_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.gru = nn.GRU(
            input_size=input_size,
            hidden_size=hidden_size,
            num_layers=num_layers,
            dropout=0.2,
            batch_first=True
        )
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        out, hn = self.gru(x)
        return self.fc(out[:, -1, :])
```

title: Variational Autoencoder
definition: A generative autoencoder that learns to encode input data into a probabilistic latent space (mean and variance vectors) rather than a fixed point. New data can be generated by sampling from this learned distribution.
why_used: Enables smooth, structured latent spaces where interpolation between points is meaningful. Unlike standard autoencoders, VAEs are proper generative models because the latent space is regularized to follow a Gaussian distribution.
interview_point: The VAE loss has two terms: Reconstruction Loss (how well the decoder reconstructs input) + KL Divergence (regularization forcing the latent distribution toward N(0,1)). The reparameterization trick (z = μ + ε·σ) makes the sampling operation differentiable for backpropagation.
advantages: Smooth, structured latent space enables interpolation
limitations: Generated images often blurry compared to GANs
KL divergence can cause posterior collapse
Reconstruction quality lower than standard autoencoders
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class VAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, latent_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, 256), nn.ReLU())
        self.mu = nn.Linear(256, latent_dim)
        self.log_var = nn.Linear(256, latent_dim)
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(latent_dim, 256), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(256, input_dim), nn.Sigmoid()
        )
    
    def reparameterize(self, mu, log_var):
        std = torch.exp(0.5 * log_var)
        eps = torch.randn_like(std)  # Reparameterization trick
        return mu + eps * std
    
    def forward(self, x):
        h = self.encoder(x)
        mu, log_var = self.mu(h), self.log_var(h)
        z = self.reparameterize(mu, log_var)
        return self.decoder(z), mu, log_var
```

title: Generative Adversarial Network
definition: A framework where two neural networks — a Generator (creates fake data) and a Discriminator (distinguishes real from fake) — compete in a minimax game. The Generator learns to create increasingly realistic data to fool the Discriminator.
why_used: Produces highly realistic synthetic data (images, audio, video) and enables creative applications like style transfer, super-resolution, and data augmentation.
interview_point: Training instability is GANs' biggest challenge: Mode Collapse (generator produces only a few types of samples), Vanishing gradients (when discriminator is too good), and Non-convergence. Solutions: Wasserstein GAN (WGAN), gradient penalty, spectral normalization.
limitations: Notoriously difficult to train
Mode collapse
No explicit density estimation
Evaluation is difficult (FID, IS scores)
formula: min_G max_D V(D,G) = E[log D(x)] + E[log(1 - D(G(z)))]

title: Depthwise Separable Convolutions
definition: A convolutional operation split into two stages: 1) Depthwise Convolution (applies a single spatial filter per channel) and 2) Pointwise Convolution (applies a 1x1 filter to combine channel outputs). Reduces parameters and computational cost drastically.
why_used: Key to building lightweight models like MobileNet. Achieves high accuracy with a fraction of the parameters and multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations compared to standard convolutions.
interview_point: Standard convolution parameter cost: `D_K * D_K * M * N`. Depthwise separable cost: `D_K * D_K * M + M * N`. The reduction in computation is roughly `1/N + 1/D_K²`. For 3x3 kernels, this reduces compute by 8-9x with only a minimal drop in accuracy.
advantages: Faster inference on mobile and edge hardware
limitations: Less representation capacity than standard convolutions
Can be memory-bandwidth bound on certain hardware

title: Adam Optimizer
definition: An optimization algorithm that computes adaptive learning rates for each parameter. Combines the principles of Momentum (accumulating past gradients) and RMSProp (scaling by squared gradients).
why_used: Default optimizer for most deep learning architectures. Highly robust to hyperparameter settings, converges rapidly, and handles noisy gradients or sparse data efficiently.
interview_point: Adam keeps track of the first moment (mean of gradients, `m_t`) and second moment (uncentered variance of gradients, `v_t`). It uses bias-correction terms `1 - β₁ᵗ` and `1 - β₂ᵗ` to account for initializing moments to zero. Standard values: `β₁ = 0.9`, `β₂ = 0.999`, `ε = 1e-8`.
limitations: Can generalize worse than SGD with momentum in some computer vision tasks
Higher memory usage (stores two states per parameter)
real_world_example:
```python
import torch

# Standard Adam optimizer setup in PyTorch
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, betas=(0.9, 0.999))
```

title: Batch Normalization
definition: A technique that normalizes the inputs of each layer within a mini-batch to have zero mean and unit variance, then applies learnable scale (γ) and shift (β) parameters. Applied after linear or convolutional layers and before the activation function.
why_used: Stabilizes and accelerates training by reducing internal covariate shift — the change in layer input distributions as model weights update. Allows use of higher learning rates and acts as a mild regularizer.
interview_point: BatchNorm uses mini-batch statistics (mean, variance) during training but switches to running population statistics during inference. This train/inference discrepancy is a common source of bugs — always call model.eval() before inference.
limitations: Statistics depend on batch size — small batches give noisy estimates
Adds parameters (γ, β) per feature
Train/inference mode difference can cause subtle bugs
formula: x̂ = (x − μ_B) / √(σ²_B + ε),   y = γx̂ + β
real_world_example:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

# BatchNorm after a Linear layer (for MLP classification head)
head = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(1280, 512),
    nn.BatchNorm1d(512),   # Normalize 512 features across the batch
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Dropout(0.3),
    nn.Linear(512, 6)      # 6-class output
)

# BatchNorm after a Conv layer (for CNN)
conv_block = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(64, 128, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
    nn.BatchNorm2d(128),   # Normalize 128 channels
    nn.ReLU()
)
```

title: Linear Layer
definition: A neural network layer that applies a learned affine transformation: y = xW^T + b. Every input neuron is connected to every output neuron via learnable weight matrix W and bias vector b. Also known as a Fully Connected or Dense layer.
why_used: The fundamental building block of neural network classification and regression heads. Projects feature representations from one dimension to another and learns arbitrary linear transformations of the input.
interview_point: A Linear layer with input dim d_in and output dim d_out has d_in × d_out + d_out parameters (weights + biases). Without non-linear activations between layers, stacking multiple Linear layers is equivalent to a single Linear layer — non-linearities (ReLU, etc.) are essential for deep networks to gain expressive power.
advantages: Universal building block for any architecture
limitations: Does not capture spatial structure (unlike Conv layers)
Parameter count grows quadratically with layer width
Must be combined with non-linear activations to learn complex functions
formula: y = x · Wᵀ + b,   W ∈ ℝ^(out × in),   b ∈ ℝ^out
real_world_example:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

# EfficientNet-B0 custom classification head
classification_head = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(1280, 512),  # 1280 is EfficientNet-B0's output dim
    nn.BatchNorm1d(512),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Dropout(0.3),
    nn.Linear(512, 6)      # 6 output classes
)

# Inspect parameters
total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in classification_head.parameters())
print(f'Head parameters: {total_params:,}')  # 662,534
```

title: ReLU Activation
definition: An activation function defined as f(x) = max(0, x). It outputs the input directly if positive, or zero otherwise. The most widely used non-linearity in deep learning due to its simplicity and effectiveness.
why_used: Introduces non-linearity that enables deep networks to learn complex functions beyond linear transformations. Does not suffer from the vanishing gradient problem (gradient is always 1 or 0 — never squeezed like sigmoid/tanh).
interview_point: The 'Dying ReLU' problem: if a neuron's pre-activation is always negative, it gets a zero gradient forever and never updates. Solutions: Leaky ReLU (small negative slope ε), PReLU (learnable slope), ELU, or GELU (used in transformers).
advantages: No vanishing gradient for positive activations
limitations: Dying ReLU: neurons can permanently stop firing
Not zero-centered (unlike tanh)
Not differentiable at x=0
formula: f(x) = max(0, x)   →   f'(x) = 1 if x > 0, else 0
use_cases: Hidden layers in CNNs and MLPs
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# ReLU in a classification block
block = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(512, 256),
    nn.ReLU(),          # In-place for memory efficiency: nn.ReLU(inplace=True)
    nn.Dropout(0.3)
)

# Leaky ReLU — avoids dying ReLU with small negative slope
leaky = nn.LeakyReLU(negative_slope=0.01)

# GELU — used in transformers (smoother than ReLU)
gelu = nn.GELU()
```

title: Dropout Layer
definition: A regularization technique that randomly sets a fraction p of neuron activations to zero during each training forward pass. At inference time, all neurons are active and their weights are scaled by (1-p) to compensate.
why_used: Prevents overfitting by forcing the network to learn redundant representations — no single neuron can rely on the presence of other specific neurons. Acts as an ensemble of many different network architectures trained jointly.
interview_point: Dropout with probability p: during training, each neuron is dropped (set to 0) independently with probability p, and surviving activations are scaled up by 1/(1-p) to keep expected values consistent (inverted dropout). During inference, dropout is disabled and no scaling is needed.
limitations: Slows convergence (effectively trains a different subnetwork each step)
Not effective for small datasets
Less effective with batch normalization (can interfere)
Adds another hyperparameter p to tune
use_cases: NLP models (BERT, GPT use attention dropout)
real_world_example:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

# Dropout in EfficientNet-B0 classification head
head = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(1280, 512),
    nn.BatchNorm1d(512),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Dropout(p=0.3),     # Drop 30% of neurons during training
    nn.Linear(512, 6)
)

# IMPORTANT: Dropout is only active in training mode
head.train()   # Dropout ON
head.eval()    # Dropout OFF (must call before inference!)
```

title: CrossEntropyLoss
definition: The standard loss function for multi-class classification. Combines LogSoftmax and Negative Log-Likelihood Loss (NLLLoss) into a single operation. Measures how different the predicted class probability distribution is from the true one-hot label.
why_used: Encourages the model to assign high probability to the correct class and low probability to all others. The log ensures large penalties for confident wrong predictions, driving rapid learning.
interview_point: PyTorch's nn.CrossEntropyLoss expects raw logits (NOT softmax outputs) — it applies log-softmax internally for numerical stability. A common bug is passing softmax outputs then getting double-softmax. Also: label_smoothing=0.1 (PyTorch ≥1.10) prevents overconfident predictions.
limitations: Sensitive to class imbalance — use class_weight parameter
Can cause overconfident predictions — mitigate with label smoothing
formula: L = -(1/N) Σ log(p_{y_i})   where p_{y_i} = softmax(z)_{y_i}
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Standard usage — pass raw logits (NOT softmax outputs)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

logits = model(images)        # Shape: (batch_size, num_classes)
loss = loss_fn(logits, labels)  # labels: class indices, NOT one-hot

# With label smoothing (reduces overconfidence)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)

# With class weights for imbalanced datasets
weights = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.5, 1.8, 2.0, 1.5, 1.2])  # per-class weights
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=weights)
```
